---
title: "Notebook03"
solutions-release-date: "2026-09-02"
---

### Setup

Run all of the following before starting the notebook.

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
#| eval: false
! wget -q -nc https://raw.githubusercontent.com/taylor-arnold/fds2/refs/heads/main/funs.py
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

ub = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/taylor-arnold/fds2/refs/heads/main/"
```

```{python}
#| tags: [remove]
#| include: false
ub = ""
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
film = (
    pl.read_csv(ub + "data/criterion.csv")
    .select(
        c.title, c.year, c.director, c.country, c.language, c.genre,
        c.runtime_raw, c.imdb_votes, c.rating_imdb, c.rating_rt, c.rating_mc
    )
    .drop_nulls(subset=["runtime_raw", "imdb_votes", "rating_imdb"])
)
```

### Questions

The `film` dataset describes the films released by the Criterion Collection, a
distribution company that publishes restored editions of films it considers
important. Each row is one film, with its `title`, `year` of release,
`director`, `country` of production, `language`, and `genre`, along with its
`runtime_raw` (in minutes) and three critical scores: `rating_imdb` (0-10),
`rating_rt` (Rotten Tomatoes, 0-100), and `rating_mc` (Metacritic, 0-100). The
column `imdb_votes` records how many IMDb users rated the film, which is a
rough measure of how widely seen it is.

Unless a question says otherwise, just print the result of each block; do not
save it to a variable.

1. Start by sorting the films by year, with the oldest films at the top.

```{python}
(
    film
    .sort(c.year)
)
```

2. Now sort the films by their IMDb rating, with the highest rated films at the
top.

```{python}
(
    film
    .sort(c.rating_imdb, descending=True)
)
```

3. Sort the films by country (alphabetically) and then, within each country, by
year. Note: when we say "sort" without qualification, we mean ascending order.

```{python}
(
    film
    .sort(c.country, c.year)
)
```

4. The dataset has more columns than we can comfortably read at once. Take just
the title, director, and year columns. Notice how similar this code is to the
code in the previous question.

```{python}
(
    film
    .select(c.title, c.director, c.year)
)
```

5. Let's chain several methods together. Print the ten films with the most IMDb
votes, showing only the title and the number of votes.

```{python}
(
    film
    .sort(c.imdb_votes, descending=True)
    .head(n=10)
    .select(c.title, c.imdb_votes)
)
```

6. Now select just those films that run longer than 200 minutes.

```{python}
(
    film
    .filter(c.runtime_raw > 200)
)
```

7. Select the films whose country is Japan. There should be 172 of them.

```{python}
(
    film
    .filter(c.country == "Japan")
)
```

8. Our dataset actually contains 198 films that Japan had a hand in producing,
but the previous question found only 172. Look closely at the values in the
`country` column. Why does our filter miss the other 26 films?

**Answer**: Films with more than one production country store all of them in a
single value, separated by a vertical bar: `"Japan|France"`, `"Japan|United
States"`, and so on. The test `c.country == "Japan"` asks whether the whole
value is exactly the four letters `Japan`, so a co-production like
`"Japan|France"` answers `False` and is dropped. Data stored this way is
annoying to work with, and we will see how to split it apart later in the
semester.

9. Select the Japanese films released before 1960, sorted from the highest IMDb
rating to the lowest. Use a single call to `.filter` with the `&` (and)
operator.

```{python}
(
    film
    .filter((c.country == "Japan") & (c.year < 1960))
    .sort(c.rating_imdb, descending=True)
)
```

10. Now select the films made in one of the three Scandinavian countries
(Sweden, Denmark, or Norway) and sort them by year. Use the `.is_in` method
rather than the `|` (or) operator.

```{python}
(
    film
    .filter(c.country.is_in(["Sweden", "Denmark", "Norway"]))
    .sort(c.year)
)
```

11. Among the twenty longest films in the collection, which one has the highest
IMDb rating? Print only that single row. Hint: you will need to sort the data
twice.

```{python}
(
    film
    .sort(c.runtime_raw, descending=True)
    .head(n=20)
    .sort(c.rating_imdb, descending=True)
    .head(n=1)
)
```

12. Finally, print the five films with the highest Metacritic score, showing
only the title, year, and score. Start by doing the obvious thing: sort by
`rating_mc` in descending order and take the first five rows.

```{python}
(
    film
    .sort(c.rating_mc, descending=True)
    .head(n=5)
    .select(c.title, c.year, c.rating_mc)
)
```

13. That did not work. More than half of the films have no Metacritic score, and
a missing value is not smaller than 100; it is unknown, so Polars sets those
rows aside at the top of a descending sort rather than guessing where they
belong. Fix the previous answer by selecting the three columns and then calling
`.drop_nulls()` to remove the films with no score before sorting.

```{python}
(
    film
    .select(c.title, c.year, c.rating_mc)
    .drop_nulls()
    .sort(c.rating_mc, descending=True)
    .head(n=5)
)
```

The order of the first two methods matters here. `.drop_nulls()` looks at every
column it is given, so calling it on the whole dataset would also throw away
films that are missing only a Rotten Tomatoes score, even though we never asked
about Rotten Tomatoes. Narrowing to the three columns we care about first keeps
the method from removing more than we intended.
